1: A type: the altar of burnt offering — 5 × 5 × 3 cubits, the largest piece of tabernacle furniture, positioned at the entrance to the court; the first and most prominent object the worshiper encounters. No one approaches YHWH without first passing the altar. The cross stands at the entry to salvation: “no one comes to the Father except through me” (John 14:6). The altar is not the end of the journey but the unavoidable beginning.
2: A type: four horns on the four corners of the bronze altar — the horns of the altar were the points where the sacrificial blood was applied (Leviticus 4:7, 18, 25, 30); they also served as sanctuary for those who grasped them (1 Kings 1:50–51; Exodus 2:28). Christ’s cross is the place where his blood was applied for the world’s four corners (Revelation 7:1 “four corners of the earth”) and the place of ultimate refuge — “we have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us. We have this as a sure and steadfast anchor” (Hebrews 6:18–19).
3: A type: the bronze vessels — pots, shovels, basins, forks, firepans — all for managing the burnt offerings. The practical, unglamorous work of sacrifice: removing ashes, catching blood, moving fire. The cross was not abstract transaction but real suffering: “who in the days of his flesh offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears” (Hebrews 5:7). Atonement required real, concrete work.
4: A type: a grating of bronze network for the altar, halfway down — the bronze grating installed at the midpoint of the altar’s height. Bronze in the tabernacle system consistently signals the realm of divine judgment bearing (the bronze sea = cleansing from judgment; the bronze serpent = judgment displayed; the bronze altar = judgment absorbed). The altar is the place where divine judgment is received so worshipers need not bear it themselves (Romans 3:25–26).
5: A type: the grating extending to the ledge of the altar, midway — structural details of the judgment-bearing altar. The grating supports the fire and offerings; Christ as the one who bore the full weight of divine judgment against sin: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us” (Galatians 3:13). The bronze grating bears the fire so the worshiper need not.
6: A type: poles of acacia wood overlaid with bronze for the altar — the altar is portable; the place of sacrifice travels with the people. Unlike many ANE religions where the altar was fixed to a sacred site, Israel’s altar is mobile; YHWH’s atonement provision travels with his people through every wilderness stage. The cross is not tied to a location; its benefit reaches every corner of the world and every generation.
7: A type: rings on both sides of the altar for carrying poles — the altar is carried by poles through rings, exactly like the ark. The same carrying mechanism for the place of judgment and the place of mercy (ark + mercy seat): judgment and mercy are inseparable aspects of the one covenant provision that travels with the people. They arrive at the same destination.
8: A type: “You shall make it hollow, with boards; as it has been shown you on the mountain, so shall it be made.” The altar is hollow — an open container, not a solid block. The hollow altar receives the sacrifice within itself; Christ received the judgment within himself: “he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree” (1 Peter 2:24). The fire burns inside the hollow; the wrath of God against sin burned within the Son.
9: A type: the court of the tabernacle — 100 × 50 cubits, surrounded by hangings of fine linen; the outer enclosure accessible to all Israel, not just priests. The court is the gathering space; the church as the community that gathers around the altar and sanctuary. Revelation 11:2: the outer court “given over to the nations” — in Christ, the nations are welcomed into the enclosure of God’s people (Ephesians 2:13–14).
10: A type: twenty pillars with twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and bands on the pillars — the court’s structural supports; bronze (judgment) bases, silver (atonement) connections above. The approach to God moves through judgment (bronze sockets) to atonement (silver connections) — the same movement as the entire sacrificial system, and the same movement as the gospel.
11: A type: the north side of the court, matching the south — 100 cubits, twenty pillars, twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and bands. The mirror symmetry: the court is equally accessible from north and south; the gospel is offered equally to all. Galatians 3:28: “there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.”
12: A type: the width of the court on the west side, fifty cubits — the back of the court; ten pillars, ten sockets. The west is the rear, the direction of deepest access (toward the most holy place); the court provides a wide approach path. The gospel of access in Christ creates an open approach for all: “let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace” (Hebrews 4:16).
13: A type: the east side of the court, fifty cubits wide — the entrance side; the tabernacle entrance faces east. East is the direction of expulsion from Eden (Genesis 3:24) and of exile (the Babylonian exile was to the east). The east-facing entrance signals that the tabernacle is the place of return from exile, the way back to God’s presence. Christ himself is the way back (John 14:6) — the east-facing door fulfilled.
14: A type: hangings of fifteen cubits on one side of the gate — the entrance flanked by hangings, the gateway framed by curtains of fine linen. The approach to God is beautiful (fine linen) and structured (defined flanking curtains). The way into Christ is neither narrow in a discouraging sense nor shapeless; it is defined and glorious: “enter by the narrow gate” (Matthew 7:13) — narrow not as in unwelcoming, but as in there is one specific way.
15: A type: hangings of fifteen cubits on the other side of the gate — the symmetrical flanking; the entrance is flanked equally on both sides. The gospel is proclaimed equally to all who approach: “there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; for the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him” (Romans 10:12). The flanking hangings frame a symmetrical, impartial entrance.
16: A type: the gate of the court — twenty cubits wide with a screen of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen, embroidered; four pillars with four sockets. The gate is wide (20 cubits = 40% of the east wall) and colorful — an unmistakable, prominent entry point. Christ: “I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved and will come in and go out and find pasture” (John 10:9). The wide, beautiful gate is a standing invitation.
17: A type: all the pillars of the court with silver bands and bronze sockets — consistent throughout the court; every pillar stands on bronze (judgment) with silver (atonement) bands. The entire perimeter of the court proclaims the same gospel: judgment borne, atonement made. There is no section of the court where a different message is proclaimed; the gospel of Christ is the one word spoken at every pillar.
18: A type: the court 100 × 50 × 5 cubits — 100 cubits long, 50 wide, 5 high; the proportions of the court. The spaciousness of the court contrasts with the increasingly restricted spaces of the holy place and most holy place; many are welcomed to the outer court. Isaiah 54:2: “enlarge the place of your tent, and let the curtains of your habitations be stretched out; do not hold back; lengthen your cords and strengthen your stakes.”
19: A type: all the utensils of the tabernacle used in all its service, all its pegs and all the pegs of the court, of bronze — everything functional is bronze; the working tools are judgment-material. The entire operation of the sanctuary is predicated on judgment absorbed: every peg, every tool, every functional element of approach to God rests on the reality of judgment borne. Christ’s atonement is not the exception but the operating principle of all approach to God.
20: A type: pure beaten olive oil (shemen zayit zāk kātît) for the lamp, to burn continually — the ner tamid (perpetual light). The oil is beaten (kātît) — produced through crushing. Zechariah 4:6: “not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says YHWH of hosts.” The Spirit is the oil (Zechariah 4:2–6; 1 Samuel 16:13) who produces light; the Spirit was poured out through the “crushing” of Christ (Isaiah 53:5 medukka, crushed) — the beaten olive at Gethsemane and Calvary releases the Spirit who keeps the lampstand burning (John 16:7).
21: A type: Aaron and his sons tend the lamps from evening to morning before YHWH, a statute forever throughout all generations — the perpetual priestly tending of the light. Christ as our great high priest “always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25); his perpetual priesthood ensures the lamp never goes out. Revelation 2:1: Christ “walks among the seven golden lampstands” — the risen Lord tends and maintains his church’s witness through every night of history until the morning comes (2 Peter 1:19).
